main_summary.summary: Scott Bates, professor of French at the University of the South in Sewanee, Tennessee, recounts his trajectory from Chicago through World War II to his lifetime in Sewanee and decades of civil-rights work in Tennessee. Born 1923 in Evanston, Illinois, Bates was drafted in 1942, attended the Army's ASTP French program at the University of Chicago, landed at Utah Beach on D-Day, and supervised military-government brothels at Avranches as a French interpreter. After completing his PhD at the University of Wisconsin, he took a one-year teaching post at Sewanee in 1954 that became a lifetime appointment. Bates joined Highlander Folk School's board of directors in 1958, remaining on it through the organization's relocation. He worked with Septima Poinsette Clark, Myles Horton, Esau Jenkins, and Guy Carawan, helped found the Sewanee NAACP chapter under Clark's supervision with Mrs. Fowler as local leader, and was present at the Highlander workshop where Horton told the SNCC students they had to do it themselves. Bates fought to desegregate the University of the South and to admit women, organized anti-Vietnam War activity through the Fellowship of Reconciliation and War Resisters League, and compiled anti-war poetry that War Resisters League published as a 13,000-copy collection.
key_themes: Highlander Folk School and movement halfway houses
Desegregation of Episcopal seminaries and Sewanee
World War II military government in Normandy
NAACP chapter organizing in rural Tennessee
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview is a primary-source account from a white faculty insider on Highlander Folk School's board of directors during the years that bridged its labor-organizing roots and its civil-rights-movement role. Bates places himself at the 1957 Highlander workshop attended by Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks (the photograph later used in segregationist 'Communist Training School' billboards), and at the workshop that preceded the Raleigh founding of SNCC, where he reports Myles Horton's directive that the student activists had to do it themselves. He also documents Septima Poinsette Clark's desegregation of the Sewanee Chapel and the founding of the local NAACP chapter under her supervision with Mrs. Fowler as Black local leader.

The account adds detail to the historical record on three specific fronts: the role of Episcopal seminary desegregation (with Black would-be priests being sent to white seminaries like Seabury-Western and Garrett-Evangelical) as a precursor to broader Episcopal Church desegregation; the FBI surveillance of Highlander Folk School and its visitors (license plates harvested from the parking lot); and the Avon N. Williams Jr. NAACP court-case defense of Sewanee desegregation activists in Franklin County, Tennessee, where Williams traveled weekly for two years.

chapters.title: Lincoln's Friend Leonard Swett
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in his home in Sewanee, Tennessee, in June 2013, Scott Bates opens with his Evanston childhood near Lake Michigan, his 1923 birth into a family of Chicago lawyers descended from his great-uncle Leonard Swett, a friend of Abraham Lincoln, and his delight that dry Evanston drove him to Carleton and dry Tennessee ran with the moonshine that once filled Al Capone's Chicago trucks.

chapters.part: A French Interpreter in the War
chapters.title: Lying on Omaha Beach
chapters.summary: Drafted in 1942 and trained in French through the Army's ASTP program at the University of Chicago, Bates describes landing under fire at Omaha Beach with no air cover, waking in a field hospital with the beach sand still in his hair, the 1,300 bombers that reduced Saint-Lo to rubble, and the drive to Avranches with Patton's Third Army as he became a French interpreter expecting to help run occupied Germany.

chapters.part: A French Interpreter in the War
chapters.title: The Horror Houses of Romilly
chapters.summary: Stationed near Romilly for a winter while Eisenhower kept his unit out of the Battle of the Bulge to run Germany, Bates recounts supervising the legal military brothels, the stark contrast between the two regulated houses serving white troops and the many unsupervised illegal houses left to Black troops, and the disease that followed.

chapters.part: A French Interpreter in the War
chapters.title: The Worst Was Not D-Day
chapters.summary: Bates describes Avranches as the key point between Brittany and Normandy, first glimpsing Mont-Saint-Michel across the bay, enduring three weeks of Hitler's counterattack and dive-bombing he calls worse than D-Day, and sheltering in a convent with a sergeant, two PFCs, and a French girl whose head had been shaved for sleeping with Germans while the enemy targeted the bridge below the town.

chapters.part: A French Interpreter in the War
chapters.title: Walking the MP Beat in French
chapters.topic: Race in the Army and Back Home
chapters.summary: Returning to race, Bates describes how the Army separated Black and white troops to the town's segregated brothels after fights with white Southern soldiers, his nights on military police patrol with a Black soldier from Lincoln College discussing seventeenth-century English literature, and the Black classmates and Howard Street drinking of his Chicago boyhood that had made integration familiar.

chapters.title: A One-Year Job That Lasted a Lifetime
chapters.summary: Bates traces his path back to Carleton, a doctorate at the University of Wisconsin on the GI Bill, and a one-year French post at Sewanee, the University of the South, a white boys' college of 400 in a Tennessee town of 400, recalling a boyhood stay in Orangeburg, South Carolina and a Black maid in Evanston, and how the job became permanent.

chapters.title: Seminaries Across the Street
chapters.summary: Serving on the board of trustees of the heavily Episcopal, roughly ninety percent segregationist University of the South, Bates describes the fight to desegregate after closing Black Episcopal seminaries had to send would-be priests somewhere, naming Seabury-Western and Garrett seminaries in Evanston and the theologian Rosemary Radford Ruether.

chapters.title: From the Fellowship to the War Resisters
chapters.summary: Bates recounts the Fellowship of Reconciliation's anti-war work, reading the pacifist theologian John Swomley, then moving to the War Resisters League in Greenwich Village, and an activist he calls John Mac whose daily broadcasts tracked anti-war and socialist action across America and Egypt.

chapters.title: A Calendar of Anti-War Poems
chapters.summary: He tells of a long-distance call from Bayard Rustin asking to use the anti-war poetry he had collected since high school, the resulting War Resisters League calendars that ran a poem each year, and the published collection of poems of war resistance that sold thirteen thousand copies and was preached from pulpits across the country.

chapters.title: A Poem a Week, Free to All
chapters.summary: Bates describes running a poem a week in the local Sewanee paper, first typed and published by his wife and later carried by the Sewanee Mountain Messenger, choosing out-of-copyright anti-war and holiday verse handed out free so no one could pursue him for copyright.

chapters.title: Hitched to the Sewanee Review
chapters.summary: He describes Sewanee as a liberal arts school open to New Yorker and progressive poems, and an English department that, though housing many Unitarians and former Episcopal priests, was tied to the Sewanee Review, the oldest literary magazine in the country, where his own poems occasionally appeared.

chapters.title: V. Gene Robinson and the First Black Graduate
chapters.summary: Bates recalls graduate V. Gene Robinson, the first openly gay Episcopal bishop, the campus gay-straight alliance, and the long board fights to admit women in 1969 and Black students through the decade, including a top honors student from Alabama who became a federal judge as the first Black graduate, as priests and alumni left the desegregating church.

chapters.title: A Union School in the Hills
chapters.summary: Asked for background, Bates explains that Highlander Folk School was the union organizing school of the South, that he had never heard of it before arriving and found in it his life's dedication, and points to the University of North Carolina account of how the unions founded it on the social gospel as far back as 1905.

chapters.title: Myles Horton at Union Seminary
chapters.summary: He sketches Myles Horton, a sharecropper's son from East Tennessee who rose from a country college to Union Theological Seminary in New York to study under Reinhold Niebuhr, alongside a radical Christian educator Bates struggles to name, the two figures who most shaped Horton's thinking.

chapters.title: From Jane Addams to a Danish Model
chapters.summary: Bates recounts how Niebuhr sent Horton to Jane Addams in Chicago and to study the Danish folk schools, and how Horton combined them with a retired Memphis woman educator's mountain home to start a folk school in Grundy County, one of the poorest counties in the country, near Tracy City and Monteagle.

chapters.title: Three in the Morning with a Bouncer
chapters.summary: He describes Grundy County's moonshine and his doctor, a 350-pound man who at thirteen had been a nightclub bouncer, telling stories at three in the morning during Bates's hospital stay, and frames the wooded mountain with its deer and panthers as a Shangri-La for a Michigan-raised bird watcher.

chapters.title: Victor Hugo Against the Gun
chapters.summary: Bates connects his leftist French literature teaching, including a Victor Hugo poem against gun control he still places in local papers, to his War Resisters League stance and the small anti-Vietnam movement he led among his students, naming the poet Richard Tillinghast, a good student who later turned conservative.

chapters.title: Kids Running the Discussion
chapters.summary: He recalls his first Highlander meeting, where white University of Chattanooga and Knoxville students working to integrate the University of Tennessee met Black students from Talladega and ran the whole discussion themselves while Myles Horton stayed out of it, and he describes Horton's teasing, martini-fueled, storytelling style that Studs Terkel prized.

chapters.title: Greensboro and Nashville Come Together
chapters.summary: Bates describes the Highlander meeting where the Greensboro and Nashville sit-in students came together and Horton told them they had to do it themselves, then recalls a later three-thousand-person march down State Street in Chicago after the Cicero riots, shouting down Mayor Daley at Grant Park where Obama would later speak.

chapters.part: The NAACP Chapter and the Trial
chapters.title: We'll Take It, But Not Publicly
chapters.summary: He recounts a national NAACP meeting where Roger Wilkins told him the organization would take what SNCC and the Southern groups offered but would not recognize them publicly, and how he started a local NAACP chapter under Septima Clark, Highlander's education director, against the grain of a cautious Black establishment.

chapters.part: The NAACP Chapter and the Trial
chapters.title: Half the Church Walked Out
chapters.summary: Bates describes Septima Clark desegregating the Sewanee chapel, two Black women walking to the front while half the congregation rose and left for good, and the school-desegregation court case in Franklin County where Avon Williams Jr. returned every week for two years before an armed courtroom the police would not let erupt.

chapters.part: The NAACP Chapter and the Trial
chapters.title: License Plates Sent to the FBI
chapters.topic: The John Birch Society and Surveillance
chapters.summary: He recounts Southern stalling after the Supreme Court ruling, a millionaire student organizing the John Birch Society around Sewanee, a restaurant owner taking down Highlander visitors' license plates for the FBI, and a sit-in at Tubby's beer house where the agents investigated the activists rather than the segregationists.

chapters.part: The NAACP Chapter and the Trial
chapters.title: The Communist Training School Billboard
chapters.summary: Bates tells of bringing in the Nashville lawyers Avon Williams and Looby for the desegregation case, the Sewanee hero Mrs. Fowler who ran the NAACP chapter, and the 1957 Highlander photograph of Martin Luther King and Rosa Parks turned into billboards branding the school a Communist training center, with Bates himself visible against the wall.

chapters.title: A Stone House Burned by the Parish
chapters.summary: He describes how the Highlander land was sold off cheap and gentrified into a summer suburb after the school was run out, the cemetery across the road where the Horton family and May Justice are buried, the highway marker finally raised, and Myles Horton's mountain-stone house burned down by a parish whose minister begged God's pardon for it.

chapters.title: The Red Dean and Seditious Books
chapters.topic: Highlander's Place and a Long Feud
chapters.summary: Bates reflects that Highlander went unmentioned for years until the Obama era restored its history, recounts his twenty-year campus feud with an anti-gay, anti-Black dean called the Red Dean, and ties his French teaching to seditious banned books like the Marquis de Sade kept on reserve in the library.

chapters.title: A Poem Nobody Will Publish
chapters.topic: Banned French Verse and His Library
chapters.summary: He describes the socialist, Christian-socialist radicalism behind a French anti-gun poem he says The Nation and others refuse to print, and a passage from a writer answering Sade that remains banned in France yet circulates in underground book series and Paris bookstores, then offers to show the interviewers his cabin library.

chapters.title: Places That Trained and Sent Them On
chapters.topic: Aldon Morris and the Social Gospel
chapters.summary: Bates engages the sociologist Aldon Morris, whose book calls Highlander and Hull House movement halfway houses that trained and inspired organizers, distinguishes him from the Episcopal activist John B. Morris, and explains why the social-gospel Horton distrusted official bodies like the NAACP and went to the grassroots.

chapters.part: You Gotta Do It Yourself, Again
chapters.title: Thoreau Fetched From Sewanee
chapters.summary: Returning to the Highlander meeting that preceded the Shaw University gathering where SNCC was founded, Bates recalls his student Richard Tillinghast driving back to Sewanee to fetch Thoreau's Civil Disobedience for John Lewis, Stokely Carmichael, and the others, Horton's line that they had to do it themselves because the whites would not stick with them, and the no-guns rule that turned away armed Chicago Black Panthers.

chapters.title: A Busload to Johns Island
chapters.topic: Esau Jenkins and the Charleston Coast
chapters.summary: Bates describes a Knoxville Black district where he, Esau Jenkins, and Andrew Young stayed at a little church, a bus trip taking Black children from Johns Island to the white-only resort islands off Charleston where white youths jeered and Septima Clark chided their manners, staying with Guy Carawan near Charleston, a closely guarded Martin Luther King lecture, and an all-day beach meeting circled by police cars.

chapters.title: Still Singing the Same Songs
chapters.topic: A Lifetime on the Highlander Board
chapters.summary: Bates closes on his time on the Highlander board since 1958 and as president through the 1960s, the field meetings in Jackson and Memphis he reported back, how he ended the written minutes so members spoke truth at oral meetings, and his sense that it remains the same freedom movement, still singing the same songs, now joining gay and Black action, with a Southern Echo leader who opens every meeting with a song.

loc_video.caption: Scott Bates oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Sewanee, Tennessee, 2013 June 20
